Wollstonecraft, Kali, and Everything In Between

# Wollstonecraft, Kali, and Everything In Between

### Understanding femininity and why modernity fails to comprehend the interplay of gender.

[](https://substack.com/@rasajournal)

![Garima Garg's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!h9ms!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2493377a-60dd-44d9-a41e-4aff3778a15c_660x1006.jpeg)

[Garima Garg](https://substack.com/@rasajournal)

Mar 16, 2026

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!6suq!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F15bcd0a4-85c7-493d-8122-445a187c1747_2048x1365.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!6suq!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F15bcd0a4-85c7-493d-8122-445a187c1747_2048x1365.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

*A sculpture of Goddess Kali, the fiercest manifestation of Shakti, in Kumartuli, Kolkata, 2017. Photo by Garima Garg.*

Every year on March 8, the International Women’s Day, women are bombarded with advertisements to buy anything and everything because well, a woman is special. At the same time, every year, discourse in news media, social media, and mass media, tends to raise the same questions over and over again— are women safe? are women getting paid fairly? why are women still expected to fulfil traditional gender roles? why are men still misogynistic and chauvinistic? are women educated, empowered, and respected enough?

No matter where you stand (or sit) on these debates, we are seized by a distinctive tension between the two genders in modern times. While the battle of sexes is nothing new in human civilisation, there is a polarity of thought today that hasn’t been witnessed until recently in history. Where the perpetually insecure toxic masculinity finds security in brandishing long, unruly beards, and an ugly amount of body muscle, the perpetually aggrieved feminist has gone off the rails screeching about oppression. Neither is representative of truth, much less desirable or reasonable. But how did we get here?

Let’s start with Mary Wollstonecraft. Widely regarded as the mother of feminism, she lived in 18th century London. She was born into a comfortably middle-class family but her father was a drunk who squandered away the family’s fortune. He was also violent with her mother— something that undoubtedly shaped Wollstonecraft. As an enterprising young woman, she began working odd jobs such as a governess, seamstress, a lady’s companion, and a school mistress. Despising such work, she soon turned her attention to literature, becoming an editor, translator, and a writer in the quick succession.

At the age of 33, she published her seminal book, *A Vindication Of The Rights Of Woman*, in 1792. She championed the cause of education for women and desired a social order based on reason and virtue. For perspective, it took the United Kingdom until 1850 to offer same education to girls as they did to boys at the North London Collegiate School. At the same time, Wollstonecraft criticised the elite women of her time who seemed preoccupied with maintaining their beauty and delicateness in order to please men of superior social status. Much like Elizabeth Bennet, the famous heroine of Jane Austen’s *Pride and Prejudice* (1813), Wollstonecraft sought respect for women as rational beings rather than for their cultivated feminine graces.

Her book, she wrote, was a “heartfelt defence of ‘the cause of virtue, morality, modesty, and chastity’”. She asserted that the “natural rights” of man were the natural rights of both men and women as gifts from God who created us. To modern readers, her faith in God or ideas of chastity may seem anachronistic but she probably did not foresee the secular and sexual revolutions that were to follow her times. However, she was radical in her hope that the “divine right of husbands, like the divine right of kings, may, in this enlightened age, be contested without danger”.

She was also not the one to spare the men who were not related to her. She strongly criticised one of the tallest intellectuals of her time, Jean-Jacques Rousseau, for his views about women, in her book. She rebuked him for his conduct within his own marriage, his reputation as an intellectual, and his character. This was no mean feat for an age that wasn’t offering even basic education to women, let alone take their opinions about the best of its men seriously.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!JIBX!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc854d77e-8f74-48ba-b755-f08336c4a25f_1200x690.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!JIBX!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc854d77e-8f74-48ba-b755-f08336c4a25f_1200x690.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Yet, for all her brilliance, wit, and independence, she was a deeply flawed individual too. When the details of Wollstonecraft’s personal life became public, it caused serious damage to her reputation. From proposing to live with a married man and his wife to pretending to be married but living with a man without marrying him, Wollstonecraft’s personal conduct was subject to intense criticism at the time. Her live-in partner, Gilbert Imlay, an American businessman who saved her from being executed as a dissident, refused to take the responsibility of being a husband to her and the father to their baby. The baby was Fanny Imlay, who died by suicide at the age of 20. Her other daughter, better known as Mary Shelley, author of *Frankenstein*, never received her mother’s love as Wollstonecraft died due to childbirth complications at the age of 38.

Her ideas, then, were also a reflection of her life. They were an expression of hope and perhaps a resentment towards her reality. Yet, the successive generations of intellectuals that came after her popularised her ideas and divorced them from their context in the process. It does not help that today ideas are discussed in form of Instagram reels. It is for this reason that feminism today is radically different than what Wollstonecraft had envisioned.

Women now have the right to vote, equal workplace participation, access to highest positions including being heads of state, property rights, equal rights within a marriage, stricter punishments against sexual harassment, and so much more. Yet, many women still grapple with many of the same issues as Wollstonecraft such as domestic violence to casual misogyny. While women do have a lot more possibilities today than ever before, they still feel restricted and limited in many ways like they always have. In which case, the question is if the answer to their problems is more “feminism” or something else?

Let’s turn to Goddess Kali now. She has been revered by Hindus for several hundreds, if not thousands, of years as the fiercest manifestation of Shakti. Where Shiva is pure consciousness, Shakti is pure matter or Nature itself. She is regarded as the feminine principle of the ultimate reality (the gender is irrelevant when we talk about ultimate reality but we do need a way to express its varying manifestations). As Sati, she is a rebel. As Parvati, she is still a rebel but a calmer one and so, she is also a householder, wife, and a mother. She enjoys her beauty and regal splendour in stark contrast to her husband’s ash-smeared body, bare-bones existence, and spiritual depth. As Durga, she is a warrior who wields multiple weapons and kills a feared demon.

And while she has many, many more forms, it is her form as Kali that is most fascinating. Because as Kali, she is pure wrath. She wears a garland of skulls, her eyes are blood-shot and enraged, her hair is unruly and billowing in wind. She moves with an axe in her hand, a crimson tongue lolling out, and stops at nothing to kill the demons that have unsettled the balance of existence. She takes no permission, gives no explanation. She cares not for clothes, make-up, or societal norms. She does not wait for anyone to understand or validate her justice. She kills and kills and kills. She does what she knows she must do to restore balance in the universe, even if it means inflicting unmitigated violence. *She does not wait for anyone to empower her, she is power incarnate*.

She only stops when Shiva lays down in her path and she feels his body underneath her foot. In other words, the wildest manifestation of nature is contained only when pure consciousness makes itself felt once again. And so, despite her rampage, she is revered. Devotees seek to appease her in their worship and consider her a motherly figure.

# An error occurred.

Unable to execute JavaScript.

*A wonderful animation of Goddess Kali killing Raktabeej.*

But now, what is the distinguishing factor between the stories of Wollstonecraft and Kali? For one, one of them belongs to the mortal realm and the other to the realm of divine. Yet, are the two realms really so separate? Have we as humans not shaped the divine in *our* image? Because if yes, then what is the gap that we can’t seem to close in modernity?

The clue lies in the age to which Wollstonecraft belonged. It was the Age of Reason, which was rooted in material reality and reasoned that the only way towards progress was through material prosperity. In this worldview, the greater your material gains, the greater your progress. Eventually, progress meant anything from better incomes, ability to hoard more resources, spend more lavishly on clothes, travel, and food, and so on. Spiritual training in form of religion, nature, and inner life was systematically weeded out as being backward, weak, or an illness. This flattened out many of the feminine traits of emotional depth, nourishment, and giving by its very definition, not just in women but also men.

It was also the age that erupted after a century of printing press revolution prior to it, which meant that now education was cheap and easily accessible. An age that saw knowledge as an important step towards progress naturally had to decide in favour of education for all. Yet, this form of education was radically different than what humanity was used to until that point. While there have been many examples of learned women prior to 17th century, knowledge was primarily of two kinds— the higher one and the practical one. The former belonged to priests and philosophers. The latter belonged to skilled craftsmen, artisans, tradesmen, and the like. Women, on the other hand, learnt about cooking, sewing, child-rearing, and more. Often, both men and women jointly managed farms, small businesses, and most importantly, many aspects of running a household. There was never the concept of a broad compilation of subjects and ideas that must be taught to the entire population irrespective of their class, capacities, and utility. But that changed in the past few centuries and gradually, this form of education not only became normalised but also the aspiration. Wollstonecraft’s legacy lies in securing this education for women.

Sadly though, this form of education itself was flawed. It did not teach its students to think independently, express themselves, or master a skill. It did not teach them about themselves, their communities, and how to navigate the ups and downs of life. In fact, this form of education actively discourages any awareness of these aspects at all. It created a class of potential salaried-workers (across the globe after colonialism), all of whom were trained in the same language for the same purpose of better standard of life. What used to be a masculine function, i.e., provide resources to family, now became the de-facto goal of every individual including women. In other words, while women are taught to look at their greater workforce participation (and subsequent masculinity) as their empowerment, it is far from the truth.

But for that reason, we need to understand empowerment deeply too. For one, empowerment does not care about our gender and so there’s no distinct way to empower women as compared to men. The term “women empowerment” itself becomes meaningless and trite in this context. Empowerment, etymologically points to an individual who has embodied their personal sense of power. What gives us power? The answer is of course different for everyone. For a great number of people being able to earn money and spend it as per their choice feels empowering. But that is only one possible manifestation of personal power. Because for some, it might lie in being able to nurture. For some, it might lie in an ability to create something meaningful. For some, it might lie in living life as per their values and ethics. For some, it might lie in being able to gain and spread knowledge. For some, it might lie in growing their vegetables, fruits, and herbs. For some, it might lie in taking care of their mind and body. There can be as many definitions of empowerment as humans in the world. We are currently 8.3 billion people in the world.

The Age of Reason manifested in response to a series of historical events but it has run its course in modern times. A single-minded focus on material reality, including our biological gender, is not sustainable anymore and must be balanced out with a clear and consistent emphasis on our spiritual realities as individuals, communities, and the world at large. Neither does it make sense for all of us to be reduced to a single definition of empowerment in our march towards progress. It will take the wisdom (and some of the ruthlessness) of Kali to reject this one overarching definition and recognise the one that resonates for us. Only when we do that, as both men and women, will we begin to understand, appreciate, and respect each other.
